State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322 5660           322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

June 28, 1982


Carl C. Hetrick
1226-1/2 State Street, Suite 2
Santa Barbara, CA  93101

Re: Advice Letter
    No. A-82-110

Dear Mr. Hetrick:

     Thank you for your letter of June 4, 1982, requesting
advice concerning Government Code Sections 87400,
et seq.1/  The relevant facts, as I understand them from
your letter and our telephone conversation, are as follows:

                              FACTS

     From May, 1976, through June, 1981, you were Acting
Executive Director and then Executive Director of the South
Regional Coastal Commission.2/  Mandalay Beach Associates
has an interest in property located within the South
Central Coast Region.  A Coastal Commission permit is
required for development of this property.

     In September, 1979, Mandalay requested a waiver of the
requirement that it obtain final approval of its proposed
project from all relevant local government agencies prior

_____________

     1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

     2/ The Regional Coastal Commissions no longer
exist.  Their duties have been taken over by the State
Coastal Commission.

Carl C. Hetrick
June 28, 1982
Page 2


to filing a permit application with the Regional Coastal
Commission. Such a waiver can be granted under certain
specified conditions. However, in practice, waivers were
only granted by the Regional Commission in exceptional
circumstances. As Executive Director, you denied
Mandalay's request for the waiver.

In December, 1979, Mandalay began discussions with the
Regional Coastal Commission in connection with the
technical requirements for filing a permit application.
The two major questions involved were (1) whether the
project had the requisite approvals from the City of
Oxnard, and (2) whether the application contained
sufficient detail and supporting documentation to enable
the Commission staff to review the application. The
discussions did not address the merits or the substance of
the project proposal. You participated in these
discussions and wrote two letters to Mandalay concerning
the requirements for filing an application.3/ You did
not personally have any involvement with the Mandalay
application after this point.

Mandalay eventually filed a permit application with the
Regional Commission. The application was approved by the
Regional Commission in June, 1981. An appeal was taken to
the State Coastal Commission which affirmed the Regional
Commission's decision. Mandalay has now requested you to
represent them in an application to the State Coastal
Commission for an amendment to the permit. They also want
you to advise them as to the optimal manner of satisfying
the outstanding conditions of approval associated with the
initial permit.

CONCLUSION

You are not prohibited from representing Mandalay Beach
Associates in connection with this permit by Sections
87400, et seq.

_______________________

3/ Copies of these letters were attached to your
letter.

Carl C. Hetrick
June 28, 1982
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Section 87401 provides:

     No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney
for, or otherwise represent, any other person
(other than the State of California) before any
court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof:

     (a) By making any formal or informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written ocmmunication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

     (b) The State of California is a party or has a
direct and substantial interest; and

     (c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated.

     It is clear that you are a former state administrative
official, and that you have been asked to represent
Mandalay Beach Associates for compensation before a state
administrative agency (the State Coastal Commission). It
is also clear that the conditions of subsections (a) and
(b) of Section 87401 would be met by this representation.
However, the conditions of subsection (c) would not be met
by your representation of Mandalay.

     Subsection (c) provides that Section 87401 applies only
when a former official is asked to represent a party in a
"proceeding" in which the former official "participated."
A proceeding includes, among other things, any "matter"
involving a "specific party." Section 87401(d)(c). It is our
interpretation that a "matter" can involve several
procedurally distinct steps, if the underlying subject
matter (or project proposal) is the same. In this case,
all the different procedural steps involved in Mandalay's
permit application, starting with the request for a waiver
in 1979, through the current application for an amendment
to the permit, constitute one "proceeding."

Carl C. Hetrick
June 28, 1982
Page 4


The term "participated" is defined in Section 87400(d):

"Participated" means to have taken part personally
and substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering
advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information as an officer or employee, but
excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal
advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which
do not involve a specific party or parties.

It is our view that your participation in the Mandalay
permit proceeding was not "substantial" within the meaning
of Section 87400(d). The examples of participation given
in 87400 imply that unless an official participates in a
substantive decision, or has access to confidential
information, there is no "participation" within the meaning
of Section 87401(c). As you have described the situation,
your involvement was limited to purely procedural matters
(and might even be characterized as "ministerial" in
nature). You did not participate in a substantive review
of Mandalay's application, nor were you involved in any
confidential agency discussions or review of the
application. Thus, you did not "participate" in the
earlier stages of the permit application proceeding, and,
as a result, you are not now prohibited from representing
Mandalay in its application for an amendment of the permit.

If you have any further questions concerning Sections
87400, et seq., please do not hesitate to get in touch with
us. I hope, however, that this letter, along with the
other advice letters which I have enclosed, will help you
in applying these sections to future situations.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


June 22, 1982


Carl C. Hetrick, Ph.D.
1226-1/2 State Street, Suite 2
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: A-82-110

Dear Dr. Hetrick:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to me. If you have any
questions about your advice request, please contact me
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,


Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh

Carl C. Hetrick, PhD                                                Land Use Consultant

1226½ State Street, Suite 2  •  Santa Barbara, California 93101  •  (805) 966-6421


                                                                    June 4, 1982


Robert M. Stern
General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804
                                                               Certified Mail

Dear Mr. Stern:

     The following letter concerns the question of whether a proposed
activity on my part would be incompatible with the requirements of
the "Waters Bill" (Government Code Section 87400 et seq.).

     From August, 1973 through May, 1976 I was employed as a Coastal
Conservation and Development Analyst II with the South Central
Coast Regional Coastal Commission. From May, 1976 through June,
1981 I was the Acting Executive Director and Executive Director
of the South Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission.

     I have been approached by Raznick & Sons, Inc. on behalf of Mandalay
Beach Associates, the permittee of South Central Coast Regional Com-
mission permit No. 217-23, to represent them in an application to the
California Coastal Commission to amend the referenced, already
issued coastal permit in certain regards. Additionally, I have been
requested to advise them as to the optimal manner of satisfying the
outstanding conditions of approval associated with this permit prior
to the initiation of construction on the project.

     Because this permit was approved by the Regional Commission during
my tenure as Executive Director, I am requesting your advice prior
to agreeing to represent Raznick & Sons in this matter.

     The following is my best effort at reconstructing the relevant per-
mit history as well as my own involvement therein. The reconstruc-
tion is based upon my personal examination of the applicable
permit file and Regional Commission meeting minutes, an examination
of my appointment books for the time period in question, interviews
with Billie Blanchard and John Lien of the Commission's Santa Barbara
office, and my own best recollection of the events. Blanchard was


 •  Governmental Relations  •  Project Development  •  Permit Facilitating

Robert M. Stern                    -2-                    June 4, 1982

the permit analyst assigned to the project; Lien was, and is, the
Permit Chief in the Commission's local office.

The project was considered by the Regional Commission at hearings
held on June 12 and June 26, 1981. Application 217-23 was approved
by the Commission following the latter hearing on a vote of 7 yes,
0 opposed, 5 absent. As I understand it, this approval was sub-
sequently appealed in the State Coastal Commission which found that
the appeal presented no substantial issue, although I have no direct
knowledge of the apparent appeal and action by the State Commission
inasmuch as my employment with the Coastal Commission terminated
four days after the Regional Commission's action.

As set forth in the Commission's resolution of approval, the ap-
plication consisted of a request "tosubdivide 114.5 acres into 16
parcels and to construct a 250 room hotel, 500 residential units,
and certain private and public improvements." As set forth in the
Commission staff report, the project is located "East of Harbor
Boulevard, South of Oxnard Shores and North of Hollywood Beach,
Oxnard, County of Ventura (APN: 191-14-15-16)."

I believe it reasonable to consider the somewhat atypical overall
history of this project before the Regional Commission as consist-
ing of three distinct, though related, "proceedings", the first two
of which were a "request for a ruling or other determination" and
the third of which was an "application". I was personally involved
in the first two of these proceedings, but, to the best of my know-
ledge, not at all involved in the third and, for purposes of this
determination, crucial proceeding.

Request for A Ruling or Other Determination Number 1:    Waiver of
Prior Local Governmental Approval. By letter dated September 20,
1979, Marc L. Charney, attorney for the applicants, requested that
the usual requirement that, prior to filing, a project have final
discretionary approval from the relevant local government be waived
in this instance. The Commission's regulations granted the Exec-
utive Director discretion to allow such requests under certain
specified circumstances. By letter dated October 11, 1979 the
request was denied. The denial letter was prepared by John Lien
and my signature was affixed to the letter by some third party in
the office, I presume with my knowledge and consent.

My recollection of the waiver request is that I was personally in-
volved in the decision not to grant the sought-after waiver. How-
ever, in terms of the Commission's overall review of the project
the waiver denial was of no noteworthy significance. My interpretation
of the Regulations' provision allowing the Executive Director to
waive the requirement of prior local approval was that such waivers

_______________________________________________________________
Carl C. Hetrick, PhD                Land Use Consultant

Robert M. Stern                    -3-                    June 4, 1982

were intended to be granted only in the most extraordinary circum-
stances, a belief strongly shared by my Commission. During my
tenure as Executive Director roughly 6000 permit applications were
processed, but only a handful of such waivers was granted. The
point of all this is simply that it would have been the granting,
rather than the denial, of such a waiver request which would have
been nonroutine.

Request for a Ruling for Other Determination Number 2: Completeness
of Application for Filing Purposes. Beginning, apparently, in Dec-
ember, 1979 the Mandalay Beach Associates began attempting to file
a permit application for the project. There ensued a round of cor-
respondence, meetings, and telephone conversations concerning
whether the application, as submitted, met the technical criteria
of a complete application. At this point there was no particular
discussion of the merits or substance of the project or its ap-
provability under the requirements of the Coastal Act since one
of the main points at issue was the question of whether enough
detail had been provided to allow the project to be judged. In
considering the request for a determination as to whether the appli-
cation was complete for filing there were two major issues: a) whether
the project had the requisite level of discretionary approval from
the City of Oxnard, and b) whether the application contained suf-
ficient detail and supporting documentation to enable the Commission
and staff to review it as to the requirements of the Coastal Act.
With regard to the "ripeness for filing determination" I was per-
sonally involved in a procedural sense to the extent of two meetings
with Blanchard and Lien as to the technical completeness of the
application, one meeting (April 22, 1980) and apparently one tele-
phone conversation with Don Davis representing the Mandalay Beach
Associates, and two letters (Attachment 1 and Attachment 2) both
drafted by Billie Blanchard with, apparently, some input from me.
These letters and a letter dated April 30, 1980 from Davis to me
(Attachment 3) make it clear that there was no permit application
pending and that the discussions were general and procedural in
nature, relating to the question of completeness, and did not deal
with a substantive analysis of the merits of the project.

From this point forward, I can find no indication of a personal in-
volvement on my part in the project.

Proceeding Number 3: Application for Coastal Permit. Once an appli-
cation for the Mandalay Beach project had been later found complete
and deemed filed I can neither recall nor find any indication of
an involvement with the substantive review of the project on my part
either at the staff level or before the Regional Commission. Although
the project was relatively large, generated a reasonably long staff

Carl C. Hetrick, PhD                    Land Use Consultant

Robert M. Stern                    -4-                    June 4, 1982

report and a "two-part" Regional Commission hearing, it presented no
planning or coastal issues of such a nature which could not adequately
be dealt with either by the staff analyst assigned to the project,
Blanchard, or the Regional Permit Chief, Lien, with some input from
personnel at the State Commission office in San Francisco.

I believe it to be a reasonable construction of the requirements of
the "Waters Bill", the Stern to Gorman letter of December 4, 1980,
the Stern to Hetrick letter of December 19, 1980 and the Crooks to
Douglas letter of February 26, 1982 to determine that the Mandalay
Beach Associates project was the subject of three distinct, though
obviously related, proceedings before the Regional Coastal Commission.

In this respect, the specific circumstances surrounding this particular project make it essentially dissimilar from the vast majority
of projects to have come before the Regional Commission. I believe
this specific case is closely analogous to the view of the Coastal
Commission's planning process set forth in your letter of December 4,
1980 to Roy Gorman in which you stated (page 4) that "We believe that
each part of the overall approval process is a different proceeding.
Each has a distinct element of decision-making and different types
of review." Based on the above, I believe I would be free to represent
Raznick & Sons, Inc. in the ways described above without fear of violating the "Waters Bill."

Alternatively, I believe it reasonable to determine that the request
for a waiver of prior local governmental approval and the technical
review of the project application for filing purposes were at minimum
insofar as the overall staff and Commission review of the project
were concerned, and that personal involvement on my part in these
two preliminary determinations does not constitute "personal and
substantial" involvement in the overall approval process on this
project.

I believe it reasonable to request a response to this inquiry within
two weeks of the date of this letter and will assume that unless I
hear from you in writing with substantial reasons to the contrary within that time frame that you concur with my evaluation of the situation.

If I can provide you with any additional information regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Carl C. Hetrick, PhD

CCH/mc
cc: Roy Gorman, Chief Counsel, California Coastal Commission
enclosures: As noted

CALIFORNIA
California Coastal Commission
SOUTH CENTRAL COAST REGIONAL COMMISSION
89 STATE STREET, (805) 963-3871
LISBOA BUILDING, SUITE 612
SANTA BARBARA, CA 93101

April 17, 1980

Attachment #1

Mr. Donald Davis, Vice President
The Barringer Company
2001 East 4th Street, Suite 222
Santa Ana, CA 92705

Re: Information required for filing Mandalay Beach Associates
     Project with the Coastal Commission

Dear Mr. Davis:

We have reviewed your letter of April 2, 1980 with enclosed attachments. The attachments included the price ranges of the proposed residential units, statistics on sewage outflow attributable to the proposed project, and the rationale behind the decision to include a proposed hotel as part of the overall project. It was indicated on page 2, item number 3 that a summary list of the project elements was attached. Unfortunately, we did not receive this particular attachment with your letter.

As a result of your initial application submittal, the January 11, 1980 meeting at our office, and the January 22 and February 13, 1980 Coastal Commission correspondence, we have received many of the informational documents and maps necessary for filing the application. During the past recent April 2, 1980 attachment regarding residential price ranges and sewage outflow statistics, the rationale attachment for the decision to include a proposed hotel in the project does not constitute a Final Analytical/Market Survey. However, based on the Coastal Commission letter of January 7, 1980, it was indicated that this office would not require that a Market Analysis be provided as part of the filing of this application.

However, as previously indicated, there are several items remaining that must be submitted for filing the application. As indicated earlier at the meeting of January 11, 1980 and in Coastal correspondence of January 22 and February 13, 1980, we still require the following information for filing the application:

1. Company Description of the Project:

   All development proposals outlined by Public Resources Code Section 30106 must be listed with all accompanying necessary local approvals listed for each proposal. Essentially this is a summary list of all projects (residential, hotel, public works etc.) in your original application submittal. This has been required to prevent any misunderstandings between our staff and yourself regarding exactly what you are proposing to do. Your April 2, 1980 letter on page 2, item number 3 indicated that the summary list of the project elements was attached. However, as indicated earlier, we did not receive the information.

Mr. Donald Davis, Vice President
April 17, 1950
Page 2

2. Hotel Design Information

The information necessary for Hotel shall include 1/8" or 1/4"
scale plot plans for all hotel facilities, location and elevation
of all facilities, sun and reflection studies showing rooms,
Corridor rooms and parking spaces, elevation to scale (1/8" or
1/4") of all hotel facilities, and floor plans for all the facilities
except for the hotel rooms, which shall require typical room floor
plans for different size room etc. Then complete drawings for all
rooms.

In our letter of February 11, 1950, we indicated to you that we are
open to the waiver of some local standards as long as such action
does your project has received better review by the City. In that
letter language, we indicated that we only can find a precedent for
waiving City review of the plan so much as it would still permit a
of the full period use possible so that you have indicated is still
forthcoming, we are open in the consideration of such alternatives.
In your April 2, 1950 letter on page 2, Item number 3, Item G, indicated
that you believe that the hotel orientation can be provided.

3. Conceptual Grading/Preliminary Grading

In previous letters and meetings, we indicated that a map to scale
showing existing and finished contours would facilitate our understanding of proposed landform alterations to the site (i.e., South
and Visual Basis). We previously submitted conceptual grading
map at a scale of 1"=60' showing proposed finished contours and
plot elevation, and grading will continue to be shown thereafter.
To fulfill our information need, we continue to require a preliminary
grading map that expand the above information by showing existing
and shaded finished contours over all the proposed development area.
This does not have to be a final engineered grading plan.

4. Parking Space Exhibit [illegible]

Based on your letters of January 3, 1950 and January 14, 1950, you
indicated that at our request we could prepare a map delineating
the parking lot, and clarifying the entire parking. It was stated
that the exhibit would show the two public parking lots and the
location of existing top tier with the open air parking at the hotel
with the covered parking being beneath the tennis courts.

The original submitted Vehicular Circulation Plan at a scale of 1"=60'
shows the public and private street address areas and the tabulations
for all residential and commercial zoned, off-street, on-street,
and on-drive-way parking spaces. It has been stated previously by
us, and as you have proposed to modify it, we would still require a map
showing the location and number of the two public parking lots and
the common and parking spaces, the open air hotel parking space
and [illegible] parking beneath tennis courts.

Dr. Donald Davis, Vice President
April 12, 1989
Page 1

It was previously indicated in our January 23, 1989 letter that we would not
prepare a Market Analysis/Demand Study in conjunction with a permit about
at establishing a dry goods retail use in a date type land use area in the
vicinity of Doral Plaza.

The residential maps as part of Exhibit "C" submitted with the Master Plan in
the Area of Doral Blvd/Palm Ave. along with the large scale detailed
floor plans and elevations are sufficient for filling the preliminary and
permits, but only if the existing use of the property is the
present use of land major on the residential units that are part of the
submitted package do not have bodies representing any of the drawings.

The information that we are requiring for filing the application has not
changed since your original submission in December, 1989. This has
been stated earlier in the letter, been shot at your local
application of filing requirements. At this point we feel that we can
simplify our requirements for filing an application which has been
consistent throughout our correspondence. After a review of the
the additional information for filing, we would have our request for (5)
working day period to determine the adequacy of the material for official
filing, unless there are special circumstances to warrant additional time.
If we receive the information there is additional questions and
require, on the submitted maps and elevations, but if the information has
been submitted as required by us, then, probabilities for any requests
will usually remain.

We hope that this letter adequately outlines all our concerns and requires-
ments regarding this project. We will coordinate the complete it of the
project and the need to provide a comprehensive analysis of all related
focal policies, our Regional Board issues and the general public.

If you have any questions regarding this letter, please call or write our
office.

[signature]
[name]
[title - illegible]

CTM/SRR/86

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR. Governor
California Coastal Commission

SOUTH CENTRAL COAST REGIONAL COMMISSION
725 STATE STREET, (805) 963-6871
BALBOA BUILDING, SUITE 612
SANTA BARBARA, CA 93101
                                                                May 30, 1980

                                                    Attachment #2

The Bergheer Company
2001 E. Fourth St., Suite 222
Santa Ana, CA  92705
ATTN: Mr. Donald M. Davis


Dear Mr. Davis:

This is in response to your letter of April 30, 1980. I generally agree
with the statements contained in your letter except for one statement
relating to low and moderate housing on page 2 which needs clarification.

In your letter you stated, "You expressed the opinion that any rental housing
which we might develop would, pursuant to the Guidelines, fulfill the
requirements of the Act as interpreted by the Guidelines." Unfortunately,
my comment appears to have been misunderstood. Based on the "Interpretive
Guidelines on New Construction of Housing", I stated that rental projects by
their very nature are not subject to any further inclusionary contribution.
In other words, certain units do not have to be specifically set aside as
low and moderate income rental units.

However, the inclusion in a project of rental units may not necessarily
satisfy the total inclusionary requirements for a particular development.
According to the "Guidelines on New Construction of Housing", a project may
be required to construct a certain percentage of the for sale units to be
affordable to low and moderate income persons. (See Attachments)

As a reminder, a specific project has not been officially filed with our office
nor presented to the Regional Commission. At this time, it is therefore not
possible to specifically pinpoint how Section 30620 of the Coastal Act of 1976
and the "Interpretive Guidelines on New Construction of Housing" would be
applied to this project.

I hope this letter clarifies the staff position on the low and moderate
housing issues of the proposed Mandalay Beach development. If you have any
further questions, please call or write our office.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Carl C. Heitrick
                                        Executive Director

CCH/BB/dr
Enclosures

the bergheer company

2604 E FOURTH ST, SUITE 2, SANTA ANA, CALIFORNIA 92705
714-973-1090

April 30, 1989

Mr. Carl Hetrick
Executive Director
South Central Coast Regional Commission
735 State Street, Suite 612
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: Application of Mandiay Beach Associates

Dear Mr. Hetrick:

This is to confirm our meeting of April 22, 1989 in the conference room
of your offices in Santa Barbara. In attendance representing the South
Central Coast Regional Commission were Billie Blanchard, John Lion and
you. The undersigned represented Mandiay Beach Associates. It is my
understanding that the following matters were discussed and generally
agreed upon:

1. Acceptance of Application

You stated that if we provide the information requested in your
letter of April 17, 1989 in the form and format requested in draft
request, you will accept the application as complete within
the level of City approvals given. If we have not the
additional minor approvals discussed in your letter.

I stated that we believe we understood the substance of that
letter and accept such letter as being your parameters for
submittal of the application. I also stated to on the lot I
could not commit that we would be able to conform to those
parameters until we had made a sufficient attempt to do so and
then considered our success.

2. Sale of Parksite to State

We discussed the fact that you had prepared the proposed
parksite subject to a reservation. The letter of an extent
to construct these improvements is unusual and not required
required by the conditions on the approval of our tenant
tract time. The discussion raised the question of what
have to make our application if we don't have the sale
that we would not have to be committed we could not on
by letter to each and then I could not make the
letter, and a letter or still [illegible]

Mr. Carl Hetrick                    -2-                    April 30, 1980


3.  Low and Moderate Housing

You stated that the staff is compelled to adhere to the Interpretiva
Guidelines on New Construction of Housing in reviewing our project,
but that the Commission has some discretion in the matter of provision
of Low/Mod housing.

I stated that we are willing to provide Low/Mod housing to the extent
that it is feasible to do so, but that this was the central issue in
the disagreement between the City of Oxnard and the Commission. It
is our desire to act as a catalyst between Oxnard and the Commission
to provide a project that will as closely satisfy both viabilities as
is possible. I told you that we are exploring the various alterna-
tives, including the design and construction of approximately 100
units of rental housing on the site. You expressed the opinion that
any rental housing which we might develop would, pursuant to the
Guidelines, fulfill the requirements of the Act as interpreted by
the Guidelines. I told you that if we choose to do this type of
development, we will keep you informed of our progress with the City.

4.  Significant Issues

Upon my request for identification of any issues that may have been
recognized relating to our project, each of you stated that no issues,
other than low and moderate income housing, had been identified to
date. John Lien stated that such issues would not be identified
until a staff committee meets to discuss the project.

5.  Timing of Resubmittal

It was generally discussed that the City of Oxnard LCP may be adopted
by the City in the near future. In this event, the Land Use Plan may
be before the Regional and/or State Commissions within the ensuing
90 day period. We discussed the advantages to us, as applicant, and
to your staff in coordinating our resubmittal to avoid placing a
burden on your staff through allocation of review time to both major
issues. I agreed that we would attempt to coordinate our application
resubmittal with the progress of the LCP in order to seek to avoid
this problem.

I wish to thank you for the time which you and your staff invested in that
meeting. With mutual understanding I believe we can go forward in a manner
that is beneficial to us all.

Very truly yours,

MANDALAY BEACH VENTURE, LTD.,
by THE BERGHEER COMPANY

Donald M. Davis, Vice President
Planning and Engineering

/lh

sections.question: Whether a proposed activity on the part of Carl C. Hetrick, a former Executive Director of the South Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission, would be incompatible with the requirements of the "Waters Bill" (Government Code Section 87400 et seq.) if he were to represent Raznick & Sons, Inc. on behalf of Mandalay Beach Associates in an application to amend a coastal permit that was approved during his tenure as Executive Director, and to advise them on satisfying outstanding conditions of approval.
sections.conclusion: You should not represent Raznick & Sons, Inc., or otherwise advise Mandalay Beach Associates in connection with the Mandalay Beach project (Permit No. 217-23) or any future related activities before the Coastal Commission. Government Code Section 87400 prohibits former state officials from representing or advising others in connection with any "matter" before the state agency for one year following termination of employment if the official was personally and substantially involved in that matter during state service.
sections.analysis:
of the merits or substance of the project or its ap-
provability under the requirements of the Coastal Act since one 
of the main points at issue was the question of whether enough 
detail had been provided to allow the project to be judged. 
In 
considering the request for a determination as to whether the appli-
cation was complete for filing there were two major issues: a) whether 
the project had the requisite level 
of discretionary approval from 
the City of Oxnard, and 
b) whether the application contained suf-
ficient detail and supporting documentation to enable the Commission 
and staff to review it as to the requirements of the Coastal Act. 
with regard to the "ripeness for filing determination" I was per-
sonally involved in a procedural sense to the extent of two meetings 
with Blanchard and Lien as to the technical completeness of the 
application, one meeting (April 22, 1980) and apparently one tele-
phone conversation with Don Davis representing the Mandalay Beach 
Associates, and two letters (Attachment 1 and Attachment 2) both 
drafted by Billie Blanchard with, apparently, some input from me. 
These letters and a letter dated April 30, 1980 from Davis to me 
(Attachment 3) make it clear that there was no permit application 
pending and that the discussions were general and procedural in 
nature, relating to the question of completeness, and did not deal 
with a substantive analysis of the merits of the project. 
From this point forward, I can find no indication of a personal in-
volv2ment on my part in the project. 
Proceeding Number 3: 
Application for Coastal Permit. 
Once an appli-
cation for the Mandalay Beach project had been later found complete 
and deemed filed I can neither recall nor find any indication of 
an involvement with the substantive review of the project on my part 
either at the staff level or before the Regional Commission. 
Although 
the project was relatively large, generated a reasonably long staff 


Robert M. Stern 
June 4, 1982 
report and a "two-part" Regional Commission hearing, it presented no 
planning or coastal issues of such a nature which could not adequately 
be dealt with either by the staff analyst assigned to the project, 
Blanchard, or the Regional Permit Chief, Lien, with some input from 
personnel at the State Commission office in San Francisco. 
I believe it to be a reasonable construction of the requirements of 
the "Waters Bill", the Stern to Gorman letter of December 4, 1980, 
the Stern to Hetrick letter of December 19, 1980 and the Crooks to 
Douglas letter of February 26, 1982 to determine that the Mandalay 
Beach Associates project was the subject of three distinct, though 
obviously related, proceedings before the Regional Coastal Commis-
sion. 
In this respect, the specific circumstances surrounding this partic-
ular project make it essentially dissimilar from the vast majority 
of projects to have come before the Regional Commission. 
I believe 
this specific case is closely analogous to the view of the Coastal 
Commission's planning process set forth in your letter of December 4, 
1980 to Roy Gorman in which you stated (page 4) that "We believe that 
each part of the overall approval process is a different proceeding. 
Each has a distinct element of decision-making and different types 
of review." 
Based on the above, I believe I would be free to represent 
Raznick & Sons, Inc. in the ways described above without fear of vio-
lating the "Waters Bill." 
Alternatively, I believe it reasonable to determine that the request 
for a waiver of prior local governmental approval and the technical 
review of the project application for filing purposes were de minimus 
insofar as the overall staff and Commission review of the project 
were concerned, and that personal involvement on my part in these 
two preliminary determinations does not constitute "personal and 
substantial" involvement in the overall approval process on this 
project. 
I believe it reasonable to request a response to this inquiry within 
two weeks of the date of this letter and will assume that unless I 
hear from you in writing with substantial reasons to the contrary with-
in that time frame that you concur with my evaluation of the situation. 
If I can provide you with any additional information reqarding this 
matter, please feel free to contact me. 
CCH/mc

Gorman, Chief Counsel, California Coastal Commission

sections.question_synthetic: Can a former Coastal Commission Executive Director represent a developer in amending a coastal permit that was approved during his tenure without violating the post-employment restrictions in Government Code Section 87400?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that Hetrick cannot represent the developer because the permit constitutes a "matter" under Section 87400, his involvement in the waiver request and completeness determination was more than de minimis, and the subsequent permit approval is sufficiently connected to his prior involvement to fall within the post-employment restriction.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR artifacts and text corruption throughout. Both the incoming request letter (June 4, 1982) and the FPPC response letter (June 28, 1982) are included. The response section is heavily corrupted but the conclusion is discernible. The incoming request is more legible and provides clear context for the legal question.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a proposed activity on the part of Carl C. Hetrick, a former Executive Director of the South Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission, would be incompatible with the requirements of the "Waters Bill" (Government Code Section 87400 et seq.) if he were to represent Raznick & Sons, Inc. on behalf of Mandalay Beach Associates in an application to amend a coastal permit that was approved during his tenure as Executive Director, and to advise them on satisfying outstanding conditions of approval.

CONCLUSION: You should not represent Raznick & Sons, Inc., or otherwise advise Mandalay Beach Associates in connection with the Mandalay Beach project (Permit No. 217-23) or any future related activities before the Coastal Commission. Government Code Section 87400 prohibits former state officials from representing or advising others in connection with any "matter" before the state agency for one year following termination of employment if the official was personally and substantially involved in that matter during state service.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322 5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 June 28, 1982 Carl C. Hetrick 1226-1/2 State Street, Suite 2 Santa Barbara, CA 93101 Re: Advice Letter No. A-82-110 Dear Mr. Hetrick: Thank you for your letter of June 4, 1982, requesting advice concerning Government Code Sections 87400, et seq.1/ The relevant facts, as I understand them from your letter and our telephone conversation, are as follows: FACTS From May, 1976, through June, 1981, you were Acting Executive Director and then Executive Director of the South Regional Coastal Commission.2/ Mandalay Beach Associates has an interest in property located within the South Central Coast Region. A Coastal Commission permit is required for development of this property. In September, 1979, Mandalay requested a waiver of the requirement that it obtain final approval of its proposed project from all relevant local government agencies prior _____________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. 2/ The Regional Coastal Commissions no longer exist. Their duties have been taken over by the State Coastal Commission. Carl C. Hetrick June 28, 1982 Page 2 to filing a permit application with the Regional Coastal Commission. Such a waiver can be granted under certain specified conditions. However, in practice, waivers were only granted by the Regional Commission in exceptional circumstances. As Executive Director, you denied Mandalay's request for the waiver. In December, 1979, Mandalay began discussions with the Regional Coastal Commission in connection with the technical requirements for filing a permit application. The two major questions involved were (1) whether the project had the requisite approvals from the City of Oxnard, and (2) whether the application contained sufficient detail and supporting documentation to enable the Commission staff to review the application. The discussions did not address the merits or the substance of the project proposal. You participated in these discussions and wrote two letters to Mandalay concerning the requirements for filing an application.3/ You did not personally have any involvement with the Mandalay application after this point. Mandalay eventually filed a permit application with the Regional Commission. The application was approved by the Regional Commission in June, 1981. An appeal was taken to the State Coastal Commission which affirmed the Regional Commission's decision. Mandalay has now requested you to represent them in an application to the State Coastal Commission for an amendment to the permit. They also want you to advise them as to the optimal manner of satisfying the outstanding conditions of approval associated with the initial permit. CONCLUSION You are not prohibited from representing Mandalay Beach Associates in connection with this permit by Sections 87400, et seq. _______________________ 3/ Copies of these letters were attached to your letter. Carl C. Hetrick June 28, 1982 Page 3 ANALYSIS Section 87401 provides: No former state administrative official, after
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter denying Carl C. Hetrick's request to represent a developer in amending a coastal permit he approved as Regional Executive Director, finding that post-employment restrictions under Government Code Section 87400 prohibit such representation within one year of leaving office when the official had personal and substantial involvement in the matter.
